question: What is polymorphism and where have you used it?
context: Classic opening question — tests if you can go beyond the textbook definition and connect to real production code.
shortAnswer: Polymorphism lets you write code against an interface and have different implementations execute at runtime. I used it in the SGN system with CDI injection — business services depend on interfaces, and the container provides the correct implementation. This is how we support multiple integration channels (email via SMTP, push via Firebase) without the caller knowing the difference.
detailedAnswer:
Polymorphism means 'many forms' — the ability to treat objects of different classes through a common interface. In Java, this happens through method overriding (runtime polymorphism) and method overloading (compile-time).

The real power is in runtime polymorphism: you program against an abstraction, and the JVM resolves the actual method at runtime based on the object's real type. This eliminates conditional logic, makes the system extensible (add new implementations without modifying existing code), and enables dependency injection.

In SGN3, CDI injection is polymorphism in action — a Business class depends on an interface, and the CDI container resolves the implementation at runtime. When we added a new integration channel, we created a new implementation and a CDI qualifier. No modification to existing code.
keyPoints: Runtime polymorphism = method resolution based on actual object type
Eliminates if/else chains for type-based behavior
Enables Open/Closed Principle — extend without modifying
Foundation for Dependency Injection and Strategy pattern
Makes code testable — mock the interface, test the caller
redFlags: Only reciting a textbook definition without examples
Cannot explain WHY it matters (just knows WHAT it is)
Inventing an experience that falls apart under follow-up
Claiming inheritance is always needed for polymorphism (interfaces!)

followUps.question: Why not just use an if/else or switch?
followUps.hint: Talk about Open/Closed Principle and what happens when you add a new type.

followUps.question: When would composition be preferable to polymorphism via inheritance?
followUps.hint: Favor composition when behavior varies independently of the type hierarchy.

followUps.question: Can polymorphism make a design harder to maintain?
followUps.hint: Deep hierarchies, unclear contracts, runtime surprises with covariant returns.

followUps.question: How does dependency injection relate to polymorphism?
followUps.hint: DI is polymorphism operationalized — the container decides which implementation at runtime.

codeExamples.title: Interface-based polymorphism with CDI

realExperience.title: SGN3 CDI-based integration layer
realExperience.description: Business classes depend on integration interfaces (IntegracaoClient). CDI injects Moodle, LDAP, or Firebase implementations based on qualifiers. Adding a new integration channel requires only a new implementation class — zero changes to callers.

question: Explain encapsulation. Why do we make fields private?
context: Sounds basic but reveals whether you understand the WHY — protecting invariants, not just hiding data.
shortAnswer: Encapsulation means bundling data with the methods that operate on it, and restricting direct access to the internal state. We make fields private to protect invariants — the object controls its own consistency. In SGN3, all entities use private fields with access only through business methods that enforce state transition rules.
detailedAnswer:
Encapsulation is about protecting invariants, not about hiding data for the sake of hiding. When a field is private, the class owns the rules for how that data changes.

In the SGN3 system, entity fields are private and state transitions happen through business layer methods that enforce preconditions. For example, a matrícula (enrollment) can only move to ATIVA status if prerequisites are met — this is enforced in the Business layer, not exposed as a simple setter.

Encapsulation also enables change without breaking clients. If the internal representation changes (e.g., storing dates as epoch instead of LocalDate), the public API stays the same.

Real encapsulation exposes behavior, not data. `setStatus(ATIVA)` is no better than a public field. `confirmar()` with validation is real encapsulation.
keyPoints: Protects invariants — object controls its own consistency
Enables evolution — internal changes don't break callers
Getters/setters alone are NOT real encapsulation
Behavior methods (confirm, cancel) are stronger than set methods
Information hiding reduces coupling between modules
redFlags: Saying 'we make fields private because it's best practice' without explaining WHY
Cannot give an example of an invariant that encapsulation protects
Never mentioning the distinction between data hiding and behavior exposure

followUps.question: If we have a getter and setter for every field, is that really encapsulated?
followUps.hint: No — it's a public field with extra steps. Real encapsulation exposes behavior.

followUps.question: How does encapsulation relate to immutability?
followUps.hint: Immutable objects are perfectly encapsulated — no one can change their state after creation.

followUps.question: Can you give an example where breaking encapsulation caused a bug?
followUps.hint: Think of a case where someone set a field directly and bypassed validation.

realExperience.title: SGN3 layered architecture enforcing encapsulation
realExperience.description: Entity fields are private. State transitions enforced in Business layer (not entity). ArchUnit tests prevent Repository access from WebBeans, ensuring layers respect encapsulation boundaries.

question: When would you choose composition over inheritance? Give a real example.
context: Senior-level question. Tests design judgment, not just knowledge of the concepts.
shortAnswer: I choose composition when behaviors vary independently, when I need runtime flexibility, or when inheritance would create a rigid tree. In practice, most of the SGN3 architecture uses composition — services are composed of injected collaborators (Repository, other Business classes, JMS producers), not inheritance hierarchies.
detailedAnswer:
Inheritance creates an 'is-a' relationship that's permanent and rigid. Composition creates a 'has-a' relationship that's flexible and runtime-configurable.

The classic signal that inheritance is wrong: you need multiple orthogonal behaviors. A `FlyingFish` can't extend both `Bird` and `Fish` in Java. But with composition, a `Creature` can have a `MovementStrategy` (fly, swim, walk) and a `BreathingStrategy` (lungs, gills) — each varying independently.

In SGN3, the architecture is fundamentally compositional:
- Business classes compose Repository + other Business + JMS producers
- There is no deep inheritance tree of business logic
- Entities use inheritance only for the JPA mapping hierarchy (AuditableEntity base class with shared audit fields) — which is a legitimate use of inheritance

Inheritance still wins for true type hierarchies where substitutability (LSP) is the goal — like JPA entity hierarchies with shared persistent fields.
keyPoints: Composition when behaviors vary independently (combinatorial explosion)
Composition when you need runtime flexibility (Strategy pattern)
Inheritance when LSP substitutability is the actual goal
Inheritance for stable, shallow hierarchies with shared state
Design smell: override method just to disable parent behavior
redFlags: Cannot articulate WHEN inheritance is still appropriate
No real example — only textbook Animal/Vehicle

followUps.question: Can you name a case where inheritance was the RIGHT choice?
followUps.hint: JPA entities with shared audit fields, or template method for a fixed algorithm with variable steps.

followUps.question: How does the Decorator pattern combine inheritance and composition?
followUps.hint: It uses inheritance for the type contract but composition for behavior layering.

followUps.question: What's the cost of over-using composition?
followUps.hint: Indirection, harder to trace flow, more objects to wire together.

clarifyingQuestions: Are we talking about a specific domain or general design principles?
Is this about a new design or refactoring existing inheritance?
tradeoffs: Composition adds indirection — harder to follow the execution flow
Inheritance gives you polymorphism for free — composition requires explicit delegation
Composition is more testable — each piece is independently mockable
Inheritance shares implementation (code reuse) — composition shares contracts (interface reuse)

realExperience.title: SGN3 compositional architecture
realExperience.description: The entire Business layer is compositional: each class receives collaborators via CDI injection. No deep inheritance hierarchies. Inheritance used only in JPA entities (AuditableEntity → domain entity) for shared audit fields — a legitimate and stable hierarchy.
